speaker: interviewer
text: It is. Klaus, the last tape finished you were taking leave of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was at the railroad station in Berlin, the [GERMAN] I believe. My uncle had come from Breslau to wish me farewell. That was my uncle Arthur who had a PhD in law and who, unfortunately, committed suicide in March of 1936, in other words a few months later. And the reason he committed suicide is that the Gestapo came to his house because somebody has reported him of something. And he had a pill prepared which he had always said he would take in the event he was threatened with arrest. And he took that pill. And the Gestapo came to question him and he died. I don't know many of the details about this, but I always liked him a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he also came? He was a business partner with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had inherited the business, just as my father did. It was a partnership. It was not a corporation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was at the station to say goodbye?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, my uncle-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] oh, and a cousin of mine Dr. [? Albert ?] [? Mark ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were setting off with 10 marks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I forgot the sum. It was a small sum of money. I had certain vouchers which I could use, like traveler checks, which I could use to pay for the hotel in London overnight and things like this. Cash, I don't think I had much. I think you could take only 50 or 100 German marks out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And describe what the train ride was like and the voyage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the voyage was interesting to me because I had looked forward to traveling by ship because my sister had told me that they always have these table tennis tournaments. And since my sister and I considered ourselves excellent table tennis players, I was just convinced that I would win the championship on that ship. But, unfortunately, the final player I was teamed against beat me 21-3, 21-2, and 21-3 again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was demolished. I thought something must have happened to me. I must have been drugged. But after the game I said to him, how come you play so well? He says, I'm the German junior table tennis champion. And he also was the German junior tennis champion. His name is was Karl Viktor Hagen, H-A-G-E-N. And he and I became very good friends later on. And why was he traveling to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immigrating. He really left. His family had a bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But they changed their name from Levy. And the bank of his was called A. Levy Cologne. A. Levy Cologne. And he knew that Germany was an impossible situation. So he immigrated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 23. 22 or 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you discuss the political situation in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: With him on the ship?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We talked about ping pong. And he predicted that since I seemed so frugal that I would become very successful and rich in America. And he said to me, I count on you to support me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. German. He ultimately married the daughter of the editor of the Wilbur Forrest's daughter, and they had three children. They ultimately lived in France. But he died during the air lift from Frankfurt to Berlin. You know, when the Russians blockaded Berlin, the United States initiated an airlift-- I think it was under Truman-- to fly supplies to Berlin so that Berlin would not starve. And this way they-- if that hadn't succeeded the Russians would have taken all of Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was he there at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked for the Department of Commerce. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Coming back to the first few weeks in the United States, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you arrived here? Passing the Statue of Liberty, seeing New York? Did you land at Ellis Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the pier. You know, the Ellis-- only people whose papers were not in order were taken to Ellis Island at that time. In fact, this K. V. Hagen was taken to Ellis Island. No, he couldn't disembark because there was something wrong with his papers so he had to stay one more night on the ship. And he told me later that was one of the best nights of his life because there was such a nice stewardess on the ship. So it wasn't a typical immigration ship which we came on. It was early.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: an English ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the other passengers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Students, American students, some German refugees. I remember I met one refugee who was very concerned about finding a job. And so was I. And he had been here before to check it out. And he was very concerned. And I was concerned about my parents all the time. You know, what would happen to them. I'm a congenital pessimist. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you're congenital, it means it runs in the family. Who else is a pessimist?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a tremendous pessimist. You know, when spring, when he saw the flowers come out, he would say this is terrible. Now winter is so close again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he very often quoted from the Bible, I think it's from just Genesis, better good name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] than costly oils. And better the day of death than the day of birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would your mother make comments when your father would say these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. She was furious at him. But my father was really proved correct, you know, with all his-- what he said really became a prophecy. What's it called? A prophecy-- a self-fulfilling prophecy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in New York trouble-free. No problems entering?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I stayed overnight with some distant relatives in Brooklyn. And when [INAUDIBLE] had given me a reference letter to the Jewish Federation, and they needed a temporary worker to address envelopes for mailing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: On November 10, 1935. And maybe 12th, but it was around the 10th. And I got this job. And I was to address the envelopes to Pittsburgh. And I spelled Pittsburgh without the H. And, therefore, I had to do hundreds of envelopes over again. And they didn't pay me extra for that. And I had to work on Christmas day. So that was my first contact with Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I shifted around. I stayed with a second cousin of mine who let me share his room. He had a one-bedroom apartment in Brooklyn. I stayed with those relatives in Brooklyn who gave the insufficient affidavit. And I stayed sometimes with this Karl Viktor Hagen, who had a room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These were the three places where I stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hope to be able to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I knew it was impossible for several reasons. One is I had to learn English first. Secondly, I needed a job. Thirdly, I was so concerned about earning a living so that when my parents would, as I assumed, arrive here penniless that I would be able to support them. I had the feeling that they would come someday without anything. And that was almost true. When my mother finally came, she came without anything. But let me go back to the job situation. I did get a job in Trenton, New Jersey, through friends of this Mrs. [? Steinhart ?]. And I became a punch press operator in Trenton. I lived with a family, second generation Polish, in a house near the plant, near the factory. And I was miserable. I was homesick. And it was just terrible. But after a few days, maybe it was 10 days, I had an accident in the factory. I left my fingers in the punch press under the dais and I cut off the tips of two fingers on my right hand. And people, the factory owners, were very nice to me. And they took me to a hospital in Trenton, Mercer Hospital. And I had some surgery there. They sewed it up. And I stayed there for about five days. And the owner called my uncle in Chicago and told him about the accident. And my uncle in Chicago says that as soon as he's out of the hospital, would you let him come to Chicago so that he can have the medical attention which he needs here. And my boss, of course, said yes. And after five days I traveled to Chicago. I arrived here on a very cold day. And a few days later I had additional surgery at Mount Sinai Hospital. It was either Dr. [? Maura ?] or Dr. Willis who did the surgery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is still now the winter of 1936?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1936. Yeah, that's '36 now. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I really liked Chicago a lot. I hated to go back to Trenton, but I had to. And so then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you here, in Chicago, that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six or seven weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you like about Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: The warmth in the apartment when I went to live with my uncle and my aunt which was like living with a family again. I liked the view from their window. They lived in Hyde Park in a street called East View Park overlooking the lake. And they took me wherever they were invited. And I liked to go to all these parties and meet all those people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I would have liked to have stayed in Chicago, but I couldn't get a job. And I took some English lessons here from a Mr. Steen. Steen was his name, S-T-E-E-N. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I went back to Trenton and they transferred me to the factory office. I didn't have to-- oh, no. At first I still did work in the factory but not at the punch press. And I remember people were very nice to me. They brought me sandwiches for lunch, some of the factory girls. And then the boss transferred me to a better department. And then he suggested that I perhaps go to school, go to night school. And I enrolled at Rider College in Trenton, New Jersey, which was sort of a flunker's paradise school. It was my type. And then he said that I am not learning enough English and then I took English classes in Elkins Park, which is a suburb of Philadelphia where my boss had his house. And then after those classes I often stayed at his house. And ultimately I almost lived full time at my boss' house. He had three daughters and one son. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was just wonderful to me. And then I stayed in Trenton. And I worked in Trenton. I went to east-- I stayed enrolled at Rider.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in touch with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mail. By mail. There was no interference with mail. My parents even sent me food packages. I mean, it's unbelievable. Sandwiches, they sent me. Salami, sausage.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sent you food packages in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, it was 1936 and there already was some food shortage. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was food shortage?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, I think. But if you had money you could buy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father's business continued?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. His business continued. And his routine continued.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you hear of your uncle's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Death? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By cable. You know, the cables always said. When somebody died, the cable always said [GERMAN] which means fatally. It means seriously ill. It was a euphemism for being killed, either by your own hand or by somebody else. And so they cabled Uncle Arthur, seriously ill. And then the letter followed and they said he died. And then I heard about the actual way he died only after my parents came to visit here in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that visit.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1937 my parents came. And my sister came with them. She then came back to America. She had a reentry permit. And my parents, I picked them up at the pier. I had a secondhand car which I bought with the money which I had been given by the state of New Jersey as part of my workman's compensation after the accident. And it was a Hupmobile. I probably paid $180 for it. And I picked up my parents at the pier and drove them through Manhattan in this old car. And my father kept saying that when I get back the biggest impression I will report to my secretary is that you can drive in New York. Because everybody thought of me as a baby. And my parents, my sister, and I-- my parents had prepaid everything. They had railroad passes from Chicago, from New York to Philadelphia and to Chicago. And we went with them. And then we went back again to New York. We saw them off. There was this farewell party at the ship when they went back to Germany. And while they were here, I had urged them to stay here. And they said no, not really. They will apply for a visa. They were wishy-washy about it. But at one point my father said to me, you know, I don't have to be afraid of being tortured because if anything should happen to me, I have this pill in my pill box which will cause me to die. And he even showed me the pill in that pill box. And I said, please don't do it. Don't go back. But he did go back. And things were not so terrible yet in 1937 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the pogrom broke out in November of 1937. You know, we just had the 50 anniversary of this. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kristallnacht was a year later.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '38?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's right. So this wasn't-- yeah. They came in '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again? They came a second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came a second time. And they said this is the last time they can leave Germany because Jews have to turn in their passports unless it is for immigration. And so I said, stay here on the visitor visa. And he said, we can't do that. We can't do that. And my uncle and my aunt also encouraged my parents to go back and apply for all the necessary papers. And I pleaded with them to say but they left. They went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel-- did you have news in the United States of the increasing amount of repression--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that was occurring in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And still your father was able to run his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he-- well, then he had to sell it. See, in '38 he still had the business. And then he was forced to sell it. And I don't know any of those details. I only know that when he was here, he looked-- he was here March of '38, which was when Austria had just been invaded. And he read on the train. And he said, this is a fabulous magazine. It's too bad that I can't subscribe to it in Germany. I would like to read it every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, don't you see the handwriting on the wall? And he says, yes, but things have to be done in their own order. I don't remember the total feeling anymore, but I just knew they made a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they finally went back to Germany again in March of '38, I was at the Englewood railroad station with them and saw them off. And we waited for the train to come. And I couldn't wait for this train to come and take them away. I found it too upsetting, you know, to be with them not having been able to persuade them to say. And on the other hand, I also had a sense of relief. I don't know why I had a sense of relief, not because I thought that they would be killed but maybe because I wouldn't be controlled by my father if he had stayed here. Because we all would have had to move together somehow, live in one room. So I don't know. Now, in retrospect, I think it was only sadness, but I also remember the other feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were at that time living in Chicago, 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was partially living. Heinemann Electric, the company for which I worked, had allowed me to become a representative to sell circuit breakers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Heinemann Electric was the company in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Trenton. And they allowed me to be here [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] three or four months. And then I went back east again. And in 1939, I came back again because Heinemann Electric allowed me again to come here for three months. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's when I met my first wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you go back just a bit if you would, I know there was a time you mentioned when we spoke that you took on a job as a tutor at Princeton University?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. In 1936 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had an introduction to the chairman of the German department at Princeton, a Professor Priest. And I went to see him. He was very nice and cordial. And I asked him whether he could refer some people to me to tutor them in German. And he said he would. And he did refer some kids to me whom I tutored. They paid me 80 cents per hour, which was fantastic. And I, by doing this, was exposed, to some extent, to the climate of Princeton where I felt like a big intruder and hoped that someday I can relive this experience maybe through my son or grandson. I did it through my grandson, who is now at Princeton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue at Rider College?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the summer of-- '37, my parents came. Then I did not. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you finally move to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, when my mother came, when my mother finally came, from Germany. In November, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at the time of Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on my way from Milwaukee to Chicago. I had been there for Heinnemann Electric. I had seen a brewery or somebody. And on the way back I turned on the radio and I heard about the Kristallnacht on the radio. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, that's when I heard about it. There were some friends of mine who called me in Trenton but I that wasn't there who were in Holland at the time, visitors visiting asking whether they could do anything for me. But I didn't get the call, nor could I have told them what to do for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your parents decide--

speaker: interviewee
text: To leave?

speaker: interviewer
text: --to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they really got very serious about coming. And they had put me in touch before with a Canadian businessman who was half con man and half businessman whom they had met on a ship and who had said that for $2,000 he would get them a visa to Canada. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we did get that visa for Canada. And that visa was issued to my mother, my father, and my grandfather, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was very good for my grandfather because he would have come under the Polish quota and he couldn't have come to America, to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: That being your mother's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father. And his name is Conitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was here.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was here already.

speaker: interviewee
text: But coming back to that visa, after my father died, his Canadian visa became invalid because it wasn't as issued. It was issued to three people and now they were only two people who would come. So that became invalid with Canadian visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know it's difficult, but if you then would roll back to-- you mentioned your father died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: How was I notified?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got that cable. I got a cable.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: On May 8, 1939. I got a cable which said [GERMAN], which means dad's seriously ill. And so I called my mother in Breslau. And I said to her, when did he die, you know, knowing. And she repeated again, he's very ill, very ill. And so I said again, when did he die? And then she said, today. And I said, how? She said, she couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't want to say it because the Gestapo. She was-- you know, all these conversations were monitored. And she was afraid to say anything which might be provocative. Have I told you already about the Gestapo visit to my house? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo came to our house in Breslau on May 8, 1939 to question my father about a contact he had made with an Englishman who is suspected of being a spy. And when they came to the house, they said, please pack a bag. We will take you in for questioning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was downtown doing shopping. And when they were there, my father said to the Gestapo men, I have to go upstairs to pack my bag. May I do that? They were very polite and said, yes, of course. My father went upstairs, took that pill which he had prepared, and which he had shown me before, and died. And when my mother came back from downtown, the Gestapo people were still in there. And they said, I'm sorry. They were very polite. We are very sorry. We don't want to question you too much right now because of this condition. But we will be back. My mother said, 'back for you.' But maybe they just said we will be back to ask you some questions. But my mother understood this to mean that she would be taken next. And so my grandfather, age 80, came immediately from Berlin and took my mother out of the house and took her to Berlin to where he lived. And I think this is about the--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have a few more minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do have a few more minutes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. When did you get those details?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my mother came. But to get my mother out of Germany was very difficult because I had to get a visa for the United States. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we did get it [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the last minute. But my mother needed some money for the steamship. Only an Italian line could've taken her because Germany was already at war. And she came with a little suitcase via Trieste. We had paid for the ticket from here. It was probably a third-class ticket. And when my mother arrived she weighed maybe 85 pounds. And she looked terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she saw me, she cried. And she said, I killed your father. And I said, why? She said because I introduced him to this man, this Englishman, who's suspected of being a spy. And I only wanted him to do a family tree. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it took a long time for my mother to recover. Klaus, you had mentioned that your parents were packing in preparation to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were all packed already.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time that your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, not everything. Not the beds, not the linens, not the chinaware. But the paintings and the living room furniture was crated to be shipped to Canada.
